debug_info.goal:
U V : Type
f : U → V
⊢ onto f ↔ range f = Univ V

proof:
by
  constructor
  · -- Prove the forward direction: if f is onto, then the range of f is the universal set in V.
    intro h
    apply Set.eq_univ_of_forall
    intro v
    -- Since f is onto, there exists a u such that f(u) = v.
    have h₁ : ∃ u, f u = v := h v
    -- Therefore, v is in the range of f.
    rcases h₁ with ⟨u, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨u, rfl⟩
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if the range of f is the universal set in V, then f is onto.
    intro h
    intro v
    -- Since the range of f is the universal set, v is in the range of f.
    have h₁ : v ∈ range f := by
      rw [h]
      trivial
    -- Therefore, there exists a u such that f(u) = v.
    rcases h₁ with ⟨u, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨u, rfl⟩

    intro hf

  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if range f = Univ V, then f is onto

by
  simp only [Function.Surjective, Set.ext_iff, Set.mem_range, Set.mem_univ, true_and]
  <;>
  tauto

by
  constructor
  · -- Prove the forward direction: if f is onto, then range f = Univ V
    intro h
    have h₂ : range f = Univ V := by
      apply Set.ext
      intro x
      simp only [Set.mem_univ, iff_true_iff]
      simp [Set.mem_range]
      -- For any x : V, since f is onto, there exists an element u : U such that f u = x
      have h₃ : ∃ (u : U), f u = x := by
        have h₄ : ∀ (y : V), ∃ (u : U), f u = y := by
          intro y
          exact h y
        exact h₄ x
      -- Therefore, x is in the range of f
      aesop
    exact h₂
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if range f = Univ V, then f is onto
    intro h
    have h₂ : onto f := by
      intro y
      -- Since range f = Univ V, for any y : V, y is in the range of f
      have h₃ : y ∈ range f := by
        have h₄ : y ∈ (Univ V : Set V) := by simp
        have h₅ : (Univ V : Set V) = range f := by
          rw [h]
        rw [h₅] at h₄
        exact h₄
      -- Therefore, there exists an element u : U such that f u = y
      obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := h₃
      exact ⟨x, by simp_all⟩
    exact h₂

by
  constructor
  · -- (⇒) Assume `f` is onto, prove `range f = Univ V`
    intro h_surj
    apply Set.eq_univ_of_forall
    intro y
    -- Since `f` is onto, there exists an `x` such that `f x = y`, so `y ∈ range f`
    have h : ∃ x, f x = y := by
      -- Use the surjectivity of `f` to find such an `x`
      obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := h_surj y
      exact ⟨x, by simpa using hx⟩
    -- Therefore, `y ∈ range f`
    exact h
  · -- (⇐) Assume `range f = Univ V`, prove `f` is onto
    intro h_eq
    -- Since `range f = Univ V`, every `y : V` is in `range f`
    intro y
    have h : y ∈ range f := by
      -- Because `y ∈ Univ V` and `range f = Univ V`
      have h₁ : y ∈ (Univ : Set V) := by trivial
      rw [h_eq] at *
      exact h₁
    -- Therefore, there exists an `x : U` such that `f x = y`
    obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := h
    exact ⟨x, by simpa using hx⟩

h v

      exact h₂
    obtain ⟨u, hu⟩ := h₁
    exact ⟨u, hu⟩

    simp_all [Set.mem_range]
    <;>

  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if range f = Univ V, then f is onto
    intro h
    -- If the range of f is the entire V, then for any y in V, there exists an x in U such that f x = y
    intro y
    have h₁ : y ∈ ({y : V | ∃ x : U, f x = y} : Set V) := by
      have h₂ : y ∈ (Set.univ : Set V) := Set.mem_univ y
      have h₃ : (Set.univ : Set V) = ({y : V | ∃ x : U, f x = y} : Set V) := by
        rw [h]
        <;>
        rfl
      rw [h₃] at h₂
      exact h₂
    -- Extract the x such that f x = y
    rcases h₁ with ⟨x, hx⟩
    exact ⟨x, by simpa using hx⟩

  constructor

    intro h

    · -- Show Univ V ⊆ range f
      intro y _
      -- Since f is onto, for every y, there exists an x such that f x = y

      rw [h₂]
      exact Set.mem_univ y
    -- Therefore, there exists an x such that f x = y
    rcases h₁ with ⟨x, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨x, rfl⟩

    -- Therefore, there exists an x in U such that f(x) = y

by
  have h_imp : onto f → range f = Univ V := by
    intro h
    apply Set.ext
    intro v
    simp only [Set.mem_univ, iff_true_iff]
    constructor
    · -- Prove that if v is in the range of f, then it is in the universal set (trivial)
      intro h₁
      trivial
    · -- Prove that if v is in the universal set, then it is in the range of f
      intro h₁
      -- Since f is onto, for every v, there exists some u such that f(u) = v
      have h₂ : ∃ u : U, f u = v := by
        -- Use the definition of onto to find such a u
        have h₃ : ∀ (v : V), ∃ (u : U), f u = v := by
          intro v
          exact h v
        exact h₃ v
      -- Therefore, v is in the range of f
      obtain ⟨u, rfl⟩ := h₂
      exact ⟨u, rfl⟩
  
  have h_conv : range f = Univ V → onto f := by
    intro h
    intro v
    have h₁ : v ∈ (range f : Set V) := by
      have h₂ : (range f : Set V) = (Set.univ : Set V) := by
        simpa [Set.ext_iff] using h
      rw [h₂]
      trivial
    -- Since v is in the range of f, there exists some u in U such that f(u) = v
    rcases h₁ with ⟨u, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨u, rfl⟩
  
  have h_main : onto f ↔ range f = Univ V := by
    constructor
    · -- Prove the forward direction: onto f → range f = Univ V
      intro h
      exact h_imp h
    · -- Prove the backward direction: range f = Univ V → onto f
      intro h
      exact h_conv h
  
  exact h_main

by
  have h_imp : onto f → range f = Univ V := by
    intro h
    apply Set.eq_univ_of_forall
    intro y
    have h₁ : ∃ x : U, f x = y := h y
    obtain ⟨x, rx⟩ := h₁
    -- We have an x such that f(x) = y, so y is in the range of f.
    have h₂ : y ∈ Set.range f := by
      exact ⟨x, by simp [rx]⟩
    -- Since y is arbitrary, every element of V is in the range of f.
    simpa using h₂
  
  have h_converse : range f = Univ V → onto f := by
    intro h
    intro y
    have h₁ : y ∈ (Set.range f : Set V) := by
      rw [h]
      trivial
    -- Since y is in the range of f, there exists an x such that f(x) = y.
    obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := h₁
    -- We can directly use this x to show that f is onto.
    exact ⟨x, by simp_all⟩
  
  have h_main : onto f ↔ range f = Univ V := by
    constructor
    · -- Prove the forward direction: if f is onto, then range f = Univ V
      intro h
      exact h_imp h
    · -- Prove the reverse direction: if range f = Univ V, then f is onto
      intro h
      exact h_converse h
  
  exact h_main

    have h₁ : v ∈ range f := by
      rw [h]
      trivial
    -- Therefore, there exists a u such that f(u) = v.
    rcases h₁ with ⟨u, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨u, rfl⟩

by
  have h_imp : onto f → range f = Univ V := by
    intro h
    apply Set.Subset.antisymm
    · -- Prove range f ⊆ Univ V
      intro y hy
      trivial
    · -- Prove Univ V ⊆ range f
      intro y hy
      have h₁ : ∃ (x : U), f x = y := by
        have h₂ : ∀ (y : V), ∃ (x : U), f x = y := h
        exact h₂ y
      -- Since y is in Univ V, and f is onto, y is in range f
      rcases h₁ with ⟨x, rfl⟩
      exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
  
  have h_conv : range f = Univ V → onto f := by
    intro h
    intro y
    have h₁ : y ∈ (range f : Set V) := by
      have h₂ : y ∈ (Univ V : Set V) := by trivial
      rw [h] at *
      exact h₂
    -- Since y is in the range of f, there exists an x such that f x = y
    rcases h₁ with ⟨x, rfl⟩
    exact ⟨x, rfl⟩
  
  have h_main : onto f ↔ range f = Univ V := by
    constructor
    · -- Prove the forward direction: onto f → range f = Univ V
      exact h_imp
    · -- Prove the backward direction: range f = Univ V → onto f
      exact h_conv
  
  exact h_main

by
  constructor
  · -- Prove the forward direction: if f is onto, then range f = Univ V
    intro hf
    apply Set.Subset.antisymm
    · -- Prove range f ⊆ Univ V
      intro v hv
      trivial
    · -- Prove Univ V ⊆ range f
      intro v _
      have h : ∃ u, f u = v := hf v
      obtain ⟨u, hu⟩ := h
      exact ⟨u, hu⟩
  · -- Prove the reverse direction: if range f = Univ V, then f is onto
    intro hf
    intro v
    have h : v ∈ range f := by
      rw [hf]
      trivial
    obtain ⟨u, hu⟩ := h
    exact ⟨u, hu⟩
